title: Setup and Hold: The Interview Essentials
content_markdown:
## Setup and Hold
Every synchronous design is built around two checks between a launch flop and a capture flop.

### Setup time (max-delay)
Data must be **stable before** the capture clock edge.
```
Tclk + Tskew >= Tcq + Tlogic + Tsetup
```
- A **slower clock** relaxes setup.
- Fix violations: reduce logic depth, upsize/buffer cells, restructure, retime, or pipeline.

### Hold time (min-delay)
Data must be **stable after** the clock edge so the new value does not race through.
```
Tcq + Tlogic >= Thold + Tskew
```
- A slower clock does **not** help hold.
- Fix violations: **add delay** (buffers/longer routes) on the short path.

### Slack
`Slack = Required - Arrival`. Negative slack is a violation; the worst is **WNS**, and the sum is **TNS**.

> Interview one-liner: *Setup is a frequency problem, hold is a fixed (race) problem.*

title: Metastability, Synchronizers and CDC
content_markdown:
## Metastability
When a flop samples a signal that violates setup/hold (e.g., from another clock domain), its output can hover between 0 and 1 for an unbounded time. **MTBF** improves exponentially with the settling time you allow.

## Two-flop synchronizer
For a **single-bit** control signal crossing domains:
```verilog
always @(posedge clk_b or negedge rst_n)
  if(!rst_n) {sync2,sync1} <= 2'b0;
  else       {sync2,sync1} <= {sync1, async_in};
```
The first flop may go metastable; the second resamples after it (usually) settles.

## Multi-bit crossings
Never feed a bus through parallel 2-flop synchronizers (bits skew). Instead use:
- **Gray-code** pointers (only one bit changes) for FIFO pointers.
- **Handshake** (req/ack) for control + data hold.
- **Async FIFO** for streaming data.

> Rule: synchronize **control**, hold **data** stable until the far side captures it.

title: Reset Strategies: Sync vs Async
content_markdown:
## Reset choices
| Style | Pro | Con |
|---|---|---|
| **Synchronous** | No timing issue on de-assert, clean STA | Needs a running clock; wide reset path timing |
| **Asynchronous** | Resets even without clock | Removal/recovery timing, metastable de-assert |

## Async assert, sync de-assert
The industry-standard **reset synchronizer** gives the best of both:
```verilog
always @(posedge clk or negedge arst_n)
  if(!arst_n) {r2,r1} <= 2'b0;
  else        {r2,r1} <= {r1,1'b1};
assign rst_n = r2; // assert immediately, release synchronously
```

## Recovery and removal
- **Recovery:** reset must be de-asserted a setup time before the edge.
- **Removal:** reset must stay asserted a hold time after the edge.

> Pick one reset scheme per domain and stay consistent; mixing causes subtle bugs.

title: FIFO Depth Calculation
content_markdown:
## Sizing a FIFO
A FIFO absorbs **rate mismatch** between a producer and consumer over a burst.

### Worst-case formula
For a burst of `B` items where the writer writes at `Fw` and the reader reads at `Fr` (Fw > Fr):
```
Depth = B - (B * Fr / Fw)
       = B * (1 - Fr/Fw)
```

### Classic example
Write 1 item/clk for 100 clks; reader takes 1 every 2 clks.
- Written in burst = 100
- Read during burst = 50
- **Depth = 50**

### Watch for
- Idle cycles in the write stream (reduce required depth).
- Reader latency / back-pressure.
- Round **up** to a power of two for easy Gray-coded pointers.

> Always state your assumptions about burst length and read/write rates in the interview.

title: FSM Design and Sequence Detectors
content_markdown:
## Moore vs Mealy
- **Moore:** output depends only on state -> glitch-free, one extra cycle of latency.
- **Mealy:** output depends on state + input -> fewer states, output can glitch.

## Design flow
1. State diagram -> 2. State table -> 3. Encoding (binary/one-hot/Gray) -> 4. Next-state + output logic.

## Three-block coding style
```verilog
// 1) state register
always @(posedge clk or negedge rst_n)
  if(!rst_n) state <= IDLE; else state <= nstate;
// 2) next-state (comb)
always @(*) begin nstate = state; case(state) ... endcase end
// 3) output (comb)
always @(*) begin y = 0; case(state) ... endcase end
```

## Sequence detector (detect 1011, overlapping)
States: IDLE -> S1 -> S10 -> S101 -> S1011. Assert `found` in/after S1011, then fall back correctly on mismatch.

> Interview tip: always say whether your detector **overlaps** and whether it is Moore or Mealy.

title: Latch vs Flip-Flop and Common Pitfalls
content_markdown:
## Latch vs flip-flop
- **Latch:** level-sensitive, transparent while enable is high.
- **Flip-flop:** edge-triggered, samples once per edge. RTL uses flops.

## Why unintended latches appear
In a combinational `always @(*)`, if an output is **not assigned on every path** (incomplete `if`/`case`), the tool infers a latch to 'remember' the old value.

### Avoid them
- Assign a **default** at the top of the block.
- Cover all `case` branches or add `default`.
- Use `always_comb` so the tool **flags** accidental latches.

## When latches are intentional
- Time borrowing in high-speed paths.
- Low-power clock-gating integrated cells (ICG) use a latch.

> If you did not mean to build memory, an inferred latch is a bug — fix the missing assignment.

title: Clock Gating and Glitch-Free Enables
content_markdown:
## Why clock gating
Dynamic power is `P = a * C * V^2 * f`. Stopping the clock to idle logic kills the switching (`a`) of the whole fan-out cone — the biggest dynamic-power lever.

## Do not AND the clock directly
`gclk = clk & en;` glitches if `en` changes while `clk` is high.

## Integrated Clock Gating (ICG)
Use a **latch-based ICG**: a low (negative-level) latch samples `en` while the clock is low, so the gate only opens between edges.
```
en --> [ latch (en, when clk low) ] --> AND with clk --> gated clk
```
Synthesis inserts these automatically when you write `if(en) q <= d;`.

## Levels
- **Fine-grain:** per-register (tool inserted).
- **Coarse-grain:** block-level enables in RTL.

> Let synthesis insert ICGs from clean enable coding; never hand-build a clock AND gate.

title: Low-Power Design Overview
content_markdown:
## Power components
- **Dynamic (switching):** `a*C*V^2*f` — charging load caps.
- **Short-circuit:** brief path during transitions.
- **Static (leakage):** subthreshold + gate leakage, dominant in advanced nodes.

## Techniques (RTL to physical)
| Technique | Targets |
|---|---|
| Clock gating | Dynamic |
| Power gating (sleep) | Leakage |
| Multi-Vt cells | Leakage vs speed |
| Multi-voltage / DVFS | Dynamic + leakage |
| Operand isolation | Dynamic |

## Supporting cells
- **Level shifter:** bridges voltage domains.
- **Isolation cell:** clamps a powered-down domain's outputs.
- **Retention flop:** keeps state through power-off via a backup rail.
- **Power switch (header/footer):** gates the supply.

> The biggest wins come early: gate clocks, gate power, and pick the right Vt mix.

title: Power Dissipation: Dynamic and Static
content_markdown:
## The power equation
Total power = **dynamic** + **static (leakage)**.

### Dynamic
```
P_dyn = a * C * V^2 * f
```
- `a` = activity (switching probability)
- `C` = switched capacitance
- `V` = supply voltage (quadratic — the strongest knob)
- `f` = frequency

### Static / leakage
- **Subthreshold leakage** rises as Vt drops and temperature rises.
- **Gate leakage** through thin oxides.
- Dominant at advanced nodes and in standby.

## Which knob to pull
- Lower **V** -> quadratic dynamic + linear leakage savings (but slower; pair with DVFS).
- Reduce **a** -> clock gating, operand isolation.
- Reduce **leakage** -> power gating, high-Vt cells.

> In a mobile SoC interview, always frame answers around performance-per-watt, not raw speed.

title: Clock Gating Techniques
content_markdown:
## Goal
Kill the clock toggle to idle logic to remove its dynamic power and downstream switching.

## Glitch-free ICG
A negative-level **latch** holds the enable steady while the clock is high, so the AND gate never produces a runt pulse.

## Coding for auto clock gating
```verilog
always @(posedge clk)
  if (en) data_q <= data_d;   // synthesis infers an ICG on en
```
Avoid recirculating muxes (`q <= en ? d : q;`) when you actually want the clock gated — they keep the clock toggling.

## Granularity
- **Fine-grain:** tool inserts per-bank ICGs.
- **Coarse-grain:** RTL/architectural block enables, biggest savings.

## Verification
Check enables are stable, observe no clock activity when gated, and confirm functional equivalence with/without gating.

> Enable-style coding + synthesis ICG insertion is the clean, glitch-free way to gate clocks.

title: Power Gating and State Retention
content_markdown:
## Power gating
Disconnect a block's supply with **power switches** (header = PMOS to VDD, footer = NMOS to VSS) to eliminate leakage while idle.

## Supporting cells
- **Isolation cells:** clamp the powered-down domain's outputs to a safe constant so the live domain does not see X/floating.
- **Retention flops:** a balloon latch on an always-on rail preserves critical state across power-off.
- **Always-on (AON) logic:** controls the sequence and keeps isolation/retention alive.

## Power-down / power-up sequence
1. Assert isolation. 2. Save state (retention). 3. Turn off switches.
... then reverse: turn on, settle, restore state, release isolation.

## Tradeoffs
Gating saves leakage but costs **area, wake-up latency, and inrush current** — only worth it for long idle windows.

> Sequencing (isolate -> retain -> switch) is the part interviewers probe; get the order right.

title: Level Shifters and Isolation in Multi-Voltage Design
content_markdown:
## Voltage domains
Different blocks run at different supplies for power/performance. Signals crossing domains need special cells.

## Level shifters
- **Low-to-high:** required so a logic-1 is recognized at the higher rail.
- **High-to-low:** usually simpler, sometimes a plain buffer.
- Placed on the crossing net; must be powered correctly on both rails.

## Isolation cells
Used where a domain can be **powered down**: clamp its outputs to 0 or 1 so downstream live logic is not corrupted.

## Combined cells
**Enable-level-shifter** cells do both shifting and isolation for a power-gated, multi-voltage interface.

## Common mistakes
- Missing a level shifter on a low->high crossing.
- Isolation in the wrong domain (must be on the always-on side).

> Map every cross-domain net: does it need shifting, isolation, or both?

title: Power Intent with UPF
content_markdown:
## Why UPF
RTL does not capture power architecture. **UPF** (Unified Power Format, IEEE 1801) describes domains, switches, isolation, retention, and level shifting separately so the same RTL maps to a low-power implementation.

## Core UPF concepts
- **Power domains:** groups of logic sharing a supply.
- **Supply nets/ports/sets:** the rails.
- **Power switches:** model header/footer gating.
- **Isolation strategy:** which nets clamp, to what value, controlled by which signal.
- **Retention strategy:** which flops retain, save/restore controls.
- **Level-shifter strategy:** where shifters are inserted.

## Flow
The **same UPF** drives simulation (low-power sim with corruption modeling), synthesis, and PnR, keeping intent consistent end to end.

> UPF is the contract that makes power intent portable across the whole tool flow.

title: DVFS and Operating Performance Points
content_markdown:
## Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling
Because `P_dyn = a*C*V^2*f` and max frequency tracks voltage, scaling **V and f together** for the current workload saves large amounts of energy.

## OPPs
The SoC defines **Operating Performance Points** (V/f pairs). A governor picks the lowest OPP that still meets the workload's deadline.

## Mechanisms
- **PMIC** sets the rail voltage.
- **PLL/clock dividers** set frequency.
- Transitions must be **glitch-free** and ordered (raise V before f when speeding up; lower f before V when slowing down).

## Race-to-idle vs steady scaling
Sometimes running fast then sleeping (race-to-idle) beats running slow, depending on leakage. The right choice is workload-dependent.

> Always raise voltage before frequency on a speed-up, and drop frequency before voltage on a slow-down.

title: CDC in Multi-Clock SoCs
content_markdown:
## Why CDC matters in SoCs
A modern SoC has many asynchronous clocks (CPU, fabric, peripherals, modem). Every crossing risks metastability and data incoherence.

## Standard structures
- **Two-flop synchronizer** for single-bit control.
- **Gray-code pointers** for FIFO depth/level crossing.
- **Handshake (req/ack)** for control + held data.
- **Async FIFO** for streaming data between domains.

## Reconvergence hazard
Do not synchronize multiple related bits independently — they can land in different cycles. Encode them so only one bit changes (Gray) or pass through a single synchronized 'valid' that gates a held bus.

## Verification
- **Structural CDC** lint checks every crossing has a recognized synchronizer.
- Add **metastability injection** in simulation to flush out timing assumptions.

> Synchronize control, hold data, and never let related bits cross unsynchronized together.

title: Verifying Low-Power Designs
content_markdown:
## New failure modes
Power management adds bugs that plain functional sim misses: wrong isolation values, lost retention state, bad power-up sequences, X-propagation from off domains.

## Power-aware simulation
With UPF, the simulator **corrupts** signals in powered-down domains (drives X), so missing isolation shows up as X leaking into live logic.

## What to check
- Isolation clamps to the **correct** value during power-down.
- Retention **saves and restores** the right state across a cycle.
- Power-up/down **sequencing** order and timing.
- No functional change when clock/power gating is enabled vs disabled.

## Coverage
Add coverage for each power state and every legal power-state **transition**.

> A design that works powered-on can still fail across a power cycle — verify the transitions explicitly.

title: Pipelining Fundamentals
content_markdown:
## Why pipeline
Splitting a long combinational path into registered stages raises **throughput** (one result per clock after fill) and clock frequency, at the cost of **latency** (more cycles) and area (pipeline registers).

## Key terms
- **Latency:** cycles from input to its result.
- **Throughput:** results per cycle (ideally 1).
- **Initiation interval (II):** cycles between accepting new inputs.
- **Fill/drain:** ramp-up and ramp-down of a pipeline.

## Balancing stages
Clock period is set by the **slowest** stage. Balance logic evenly across stages; an unbalanced stage wastes the gains.

## Throughput math
For `N` items through a `k`-stage pipeline: `cycles = N + k - 1`. As `N` grows, rate approaches 1/clock.

> Pipelining trades latency and area for throughput and frequency — quantify all three in interviews.

title: Pipeline Hazards and Forwarding
content_markdown:
## Three hazard classes
- **Structural:** two stages need the same resource (e.g., single memory port).
- **Data:** an instruction needs a result not yet written back (RAW is the common one).
- **Control:** branches change the PC after later instructions have entered.

## Data-hazard fixes
- **Forwarding/bypassing:** route a result from a later stage (EX/MEM, MEM/WB) back to where it is needed, avoiding a stall.
- **Stall (bubble):** when forwarding cannot cover it (e.g., load-use), insert a bubble for one cycle.

## Control-hazard fixes
- **Branch prediction** + flush on misprediction.
- **Delay slots** (older ISAs).

## Hazard-detection logic
Compare source registers of the instruction in decode against destination registers in later stages; assert stall or select a forwarding path.

> Forward whenever you can, stall only when you must — that is the core of pipeline performance.

title: Memory Hierarchy and Caches
content_markdown:
## The hierarchy
Registers -> L1 -> L2 -> L3 -> DRAM. Each level is larger but slower; caches exploit **locality** (temporal and spatial).

## Cache organization
- **Direct-mapped, set-associative, fully-associative.**
- Address splits into **tag | index | offset**.
- **Replacement:** LRU, pseudo-LRU, random.
- **Write policy:** write-through vs write-back; write-allocate vs no-allocate.

## Performance
```
AMAT = Hit_time + Miss_rate * Miss_penalty
```
The three Cs of misses: **Compulsory, Capacity, Conflict** (associativity reduces conflict misses).

## Coherence (multi-core)
**MESI**-style protocols keep per-core caches consistent (Modified/Exclusive/Shared/Invalid).

> Know how an address maps to tag/index/offset and how to compute AMAT — both are classic questions.

title: Arbiters and Round-Robin Scheduling
content_markdown:
## The problem
Many requesters, one shared resource (bus, port, memory). An **arbiter** grants one per cycle while preventing starvation.

## Schemes
- **Fixed priority:** simple, but low-priority requesters can **starve**.
- **Round-robin:** rotate the highest priority each grant -> fair.
- **Weighted round-robin:** bias bandwidth by class.
- **Matrix/LRU arbiter:** grant the least-recently-served.

## Round-robin sketch
Mask requests below the last-granted pointer, find the next set bit (priority encoder); if none above, wrap around. Update the pointer to one past the grant.

## Properties to state
- **Fairness** (no starvation), **work-conserving** (never idle while a request waits), and **single-grant** safety.

> A clean round-robin = mask + priority-encode + wrap; be ready to draw it on the whiteboard.

title: FIFOs and Flow Control
content_markdown:
## FIFO roles
- **Buffering** rate mismatch and bursts.
- **CDC** between asynchronous clocks (async FIFO).

## Synchronous FIFO
Circular buffer with write/read pointers; `full` and `empty` from pointer comparison. A common scheme uses an extra MSB on each pointer to distinguish full from empty.

## Async FIFO
Write and read pointers live in different clock domains. Convert each pointer to **Gray code** and synchronize across (only one bit changes, so a metastable sample yields either the old or new value — both safe).

## Flow control
- **Valid/ready** (AXI-style) backpressure.
- **Credit-based** flow control for long links: a sender holds credits equal to far-end buffer space.

## Depth
`Depth >= burst * (1 - read_rate/write_rate)`, rounded up (often to a power of two).

> Async FIFO + Gray pointers is the canonical safe data crossing; know why Gray code is essential.

title: Datapath Design: Adders and Multipliers
content_markdown:
## Adders (latency vs area)
- **Ripple-carry:** smallest, slowest (carry ripples O(n)).
- **Carry-lookahead (CLA):** computes carries in parallel, O(log n) with generate/propagate.
- **Carry-select / carry-skip:** speed/area middle ground.
- **Parallel-prefix (Kogge-Stone, Brent-Kung):** fast wide adders in high-performance datapaths.

## Multipliers
- **Array multiplier:** regular, larger latency.
- **Booth encoding:** halves partial products for signed multiply.
- **Wallace/Dadda tree:** compress partial products with carry-save adders for speed.
- Final stage: one fast carry-propagate adder.

## Carry-save
Keep sum and carry separate through a chain of additions; resolve once at the end — key for accumulators and multiplier trees.

> Match the adder/multiplier microarchitecture to the timing budget; do not default to ripple-carry in a fast path.

title: Parallelism: SIMD and SIMT
content_markdown:
## Throughput machines
GPUs and vector units hide latency with **massive parallelism** instead of deep speculation.

## SIMD vs SIMT
- **SIMD:** one instruction operates on a vector of data lanes.
- **SIMT:** many scalar **threads** grouped (a warp/wavefront) execute the same instruction in lockstep; the hardware masks inactive lanes on divergence.

## Performance factors
- **Divergence:** when threads in a group take different branches, both paths serialize — minimize it.
- **Memory coalescing:** combine adjacent thread accesses into wide bursts.
- **Occupancy:** ratio of active thread groups to the hardware max; limited by registers/shared memory.
- **Bank conflicts:** serialized shared-memory accesses to the same bank.

> Throughput hardware wins by keeping many groups in flight to hide memory latency — keep divergence and conflicts low.

title: High-Performance RTL Techniques
content_markdown:
## Timing-driven RTL
- **Pipelining:** register long paths to raise fmax.
- **Retiming:** move registers across logic to balance stages (tool or manual).
- **Replication:** duplicate high-fanout drivers to cut load/skew.
- **Restructuring:** balance logic depth; avoid long priority chains.

## Parameterized, reusable RTL
```verilog
module pipe_adder #(parameter W=8, N=4)(
  input clk, input [W-1:0] a, b, output [W:0] sum);
  // generate N registered stages
endmodule
```
Use `parameter` + `generate` for width/stage scaling.

## Avoiding bottlenecks
- Break wide priority encoders into trees.
- Use carry-save / prefix adders in hot paths.
- Register module boundaries for clean timing closure.

## Verify after optimizing
Retiming/pipelining changes latency — update reference models, checkers, and assertions accordingly.

> Pipeline, retime, replicate, restructure: the four levers for closing timing without changing function.
